name: Tyler Brennan
chief_complaint: I woke up at 2 AM with the worst pain of my life in my side. It comes in waves and I thought I was dying. I can't stop moving, I can't get comfortable.
onset_description: Woke from sleep at 2 AM with sudden onset severe left flank pain that radiates to his groin. Pain is colicky, coming in intense waves. He has been pacing, unable to find any comfortable position. Has nausea and vomited twice. This is his second episode of kidney stones — he had one stone 5 years ago that he passed spontaneously.

flank_pain.description: It's the worst pain I've ever felt. It's this deep, crampy pain in my left side that comes in waves. One minute it's bearable and the next I want to scream. I know it's a kidney stone — I had one before — but this is way worse.
flank_pain.onset: 4 hours ago, sudden onset from sleep
flank_pain.character: severe colicky pain with crescendo-decrescendo pattern, lasting 20-40 minutes per wave with brief periods of reduced intensity
flank_pain.radiation: radiates from left flank anteriorly to left lower quadrant and into left groin and testicle
aggravating_factors: nothing specific makes it worse — it comes and goes on its own
lying still (psychological — feels need to move)

nausea_and_vomiting.description: I've thrown up twice. The pain is so bad it makes me sick. I still feel like I could puke again.
nausea_and_vomiting.character: severe nausea with intermittent non-bloody emesis, worsens during pain peaks

hematuria.description: My urine is pink. I noticed it when I tried to go to the bathroom. It kind of freaked me out.

urinary_urgency_and_frequency.description: I keep feeling like I need to go but when I try, barely anything comes out. And it stings when I do go.
urinary_urgency_and_frequency.character: urgency with small frequent voids, mild dysuria as stone approaches bladder

restlessness.description: I can't sit still. I can't lie down. I've been pacing my apartment for hours. There is no comfortable position. I tried every position imaginable.
restlessness.character: extreme restlessness, inability to find comfortable position — classic renal colic behavior (unlike peritonitis where patients lie still)

testicular_pain.description: The pain shoots down into my left testicle. It's a dull ache that comes with the flank pain waves.
testicular_pain.character: referred pain to left testicle, coincides with colicky episodes

symptoms_absent: back pain at rest between episodes

labs.BMP: Na 140, K 4.2, Cr 1.1, BUN 18, Glucose 108, Cl 102, CO2 24 - within normal limits
labs.CBC: WBC 11.4 (mildly elevated, stress response), Hgb 15.2, Plt 242
labs.urinalysis: >50 RBC/hpf (many red blood cells), 2-5 WBC/hpf, no bacteria, no casts, pH 5.5 (acidic), specific gravity 1.028
labs.CT_abdomen_pelvis_noncontrast: 7mm obstructing calculus at the left ureterovesicular junction (UVJ) with moderate left hydroureter and mild left hydronephrosis. No perinephric stranding or abscess. Right kidney normal. No alternative pathology.

past_medical_history: Nephrolithiasis (one prior episode 5 years ago, right-sided, 4mm calcium oxalate stone, passed spontaneously)
surgical_history: Arthroscopic left shoulder repair (2016, rotator cuff)
medications: Vitamin C 1000mg daily (HIGH DOSE — increases oxalate excretion and stone risk)

social_history.alcohol: 3-4 craft beers per week, more on weekends during football season
social_history.occupation: Fire lieutenant, 24-hour shift schedule. Often dehydrated during long shifts.
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two children (ages 5 and 3) in a house. Wife is a nurse at a different hospital.
social_history.exercise: Works out at the fire station gym regularly. Runs, lifts weights. Very physically active.
social_history.diet: High protein diet (protein shakes, grilled chicken, red meat 4-5 times per week). Drinks 3-4 cups of coffee daily. Admits to not drinking enough water, especially during shifts. Eats a lot of spinach and nuts (high oxalate foods).

conditions.father: Kidney stones (multiple episodes), gout, hypertension, alive at 66

personality: Tough and accustomed to handling emergencies for others but struggling when he is the patient. Frustrated and impatient with the wait for pain relief. Knows what he has and wants it treated quickly. Not rude but clearly in extreme distress. Between pain waves, he is personable and has a good sense of humor.
communication_style: Direct and no-nonsense. Gives clear, concise answers when pain allows. During pain peaks, can only give one-word answers or moans. Since his wife is a nurse, uses some medical terminology. Asks specific questions about his CT results and treatment plan. Wants to know when he can go home. Gets irritated if he feels pain management is delayed.
pain_description_style: Rates pain 10/10 during peaks without hesitation. Compares it to being stabbed and twisted. Notes the colicky (wave-like) pattern from his prior experience. Between waves, becomes more conversational and says 'it's better right now but it'll come back.' Grips the bedrail, writhes, and paces during peaks.

critical_findings: 7mm obstructing stone at left UVJ — borderline for spontaneous passage (stones >6mm have <20% chance of spontaneous passage)
Moderate hydroureter and mild hydronephrosis indicating obstruction
No signs of infection (afebrile, no pyuria) — important because infected obstructed kidney is a urologic emergency
History of prior calcium oxalate stone — this is likely a recurrence
Multiple dietary and lifestyle risk factors: high protein, high oxalate foods (spinach, nuts), high vitamin C (1000mg increases urinary oxalate), chronic dehydration, high purine intake
Elevated uric acid (8.2) and history of gout — may have mixed or uric acid stone component
High-normal calcium (10.4) — PTH should be checked to rule out primary hyperparathyroidism
Strong family history of nephrolithiasis (father, brother, uncle)
Gross hematuria with significant microscopic blood confirming stone irritation
rubric.diagnosis: Acute left nephrolithiasis (kidney stone) with 7mm obstructing calculus at left ureterovesicular junction
differential_diagnoses: Appendicitis (right-sided would be more typical, this is left-sided)
Testicular torsion (normal testicular exam, referred pain pattern)
Musculoskeletal back pain (no position of comfort, colicky — not MSK)
recommended_interventions: Aggressive IV pain management: ketorolac 30mg IV (first-line NSAID for renal colic) + opioid for breakthrough (morphine or hydromorphone)
IV ondansetron 4mg for nausea and vomiting
IV normal saline for hydration (but excessive hydration does not speed passage)
Urology consult for 7mm stone — likely will need intervention (ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy or extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy)
Strain all urine for stone collection and composition analysis
Alpha-blocker therapy (tamsulosin 0.4mg daily) for medical expulsive therapy if trial of passage is attempted
24-hour urine collection as outpatient for metabolic stone workup
STOP vitamin C 1000mg — increases urinary oxalate and stone risk
Dietary counseling: reduce animal protein, reduce high-oxalate foods, increase fluid intake to >2.5L/day, reduce sodium
Follow up PTH result — rule out primary hyperparathyroidism given high-normal calcium and recurrent stones
Continue allopurinol for uric acid management
Repeat imaging in 2-4 weeks if trial of passage is attempted to confirm stone movement
Return precautions: fever, inability to urinate, worsening pain, persistent vomiting (signs of infected obstructed stone — urologic emergency)
